comments.text: Small convention question: under the literal positive-integer definition\[ \sigma(A)=\inf_{n\geq 1}\frac{|A\cap[1,n]|}{n}, \]the set\[ B=\{2^m3^n:m,n\geq 0\} \]as written does not contain \(0\). Then for any set \(C\) with \(\sigma(C)>0\), we must have \(1\in C\). Since the least element of \(B\) is \(1\), the sumset \(B+C\) misses \(1\), and hence\[ \sigma(B+C)=0. \]So I assume the intended question is the normalized/nontrivial one, for example whether \(B\cup\{0\}\) is an essential component. Is that the intended convention?

I believe so, yes; when dealing with the Schnirelmann density of sumsets, the appropriate sumset (sometimes denoted with $A \oplus B$) is generally defined as $$\{a+b : a \in A \cup \{0\}, b \in B \cup \{0\}\}.$$
